subject: Chemistry
title: Lavoisier: Chemistry Gets Precise
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Lavoisier: Chemistry Gets Precise.

hook.text: Antoine Lavoisier weighed everything. He proved that mass is conserved in reactions—ending phlogiston theory.

buildup.text: Before Lavoisier, chemists thought burning released a substance called 'phlogiston.' He showed it absorbed oxygen instead.

discovery.text: By careful measurement, he proved that the total mass before and after a reaction is always the same.

twist.text: He named oxygen and hydrogen, wrote the first modern chemistry textbook, and created the first systematic nomenclature.

climax.text: During the French Revolution, he was guillotined. The judge said: 'The Republic has no need of scientists.'

punchline.text: Weighed every reaction, named the elements, lost his head. Chemistry's tragic hero.

quiz.question: What key principle did Lavoisier establish by weighing reactions?
options: Conservation of mass—total mass stays the same before and after a reaction
Energy cannot be created or destroyed
All elements have a fixed atomic weight
